## [03:00 - 06:01]

And what happens is like, if you ever sat through a presentation, like a PowerPoint presentation or something, maybe it was back when you were in college or at school, and it's like all of this information and complicated graphs. And by the end you get to the end and you're like, oh, you know, that's the feeling you're giving your prospects when you give them an Anvil Drop pitch. Okay. And it doesn't necessarily mean you're over explaining the pitch. It could be, but it's more of like the way it's delivered to where you just lay it on them all at once. And you're like, at the very end, you're like, oh, does that make sense? What questions do you have? You know, so when you do that, you will get so many, even you can literally do everything right on the call, but you'll get a ton of think about it. And if you're somebody too, to where, you know, you get a lot of think about it and they come back and buy the next day, this could be why as well. So what should you do? I kind of alluded to it. You should really structure your pitch in a way that creates dialogue. Okay. That is 100% key because then we're going to keep it very high level. We'll keep it in the limbic system of the brain and it will, it'll really position it to where by the time you get to the temperature check, they're going to be 100% bought in that this is the right thing. And it's really just money that we have to discuss. So to preface this, you should have like three to when you explain what you do, you should explain it through a process of three to five high level outcomes of things you're going to get done. So very over benefit rich. But after you explain each part, like the first thing you might do with a client, I'll just explain the first thing we do with the client. So for our sales team, build offers where we help entrepreneurs get off the phone and be able to build and scale a sales team. The first thing I help them do is establish their sales systems. Okay. Which is really making sure they have all the right foundations so they can successfully bring on a sales rep and that sales reps put in a position to win. So after I explain that part of the process, I'm going to say, does that make sense? Now a lot of people do that, but you need to take it one step further and do a double tie down. Okay. And I like my second tie down to be one that helps create dialogue. So I have a few favorite ones. One of them, I'll say, does that make sense? They'll say yes. And then I'll say, gotcha. So based on what we talked about earlier in terms of some of the systems you have in place, what are your thoughts on that part of the process specifically? So now I've created dialogue and I can get a good, a real temperature check and read of if he feels like he needs that process, if he feels like it's going to help, what are his thoughts, all of that stuff. So the reason you can't just say, does that make sense? Is because it's so, it's said so much that you almost get that reflex yes. And what we really need to do is break their pattern so that it creates some space for him to actually or her to actually think about the offer or that part of the offer and say, okay, yeah, that sounds like what I need. Or maybe that doesn't sound like what I need. And I almost want those objections during the pitch.

## [06:01 - 09:02]

And people might think, oh, this could derail your pitch. And it's like, yeah, it could. But if you don't see this stuff out earlier on the call, you're fricking screwed. So a few of my favorite double tie downs, I'll say, does that make sense? And then I'll say, based on what we talked about earlier about X, Y, and Z, what are your thoughts on that part of the process specifically? Another one I use, probably the second most often, is gotcha. And so what questions do you have on that part specifically? I don't say any questions on that. I know that's a weird thing, but it's a weird linguistic kind of hack there. But if you say any, and you can test this guys, if you say any questions on that, they'll say no. If you say what questions do you have on that specifically, they'll give you a question. And I want the questions. Or a lot of times when I say what questions, what questions do you have on that, even if they really don't, it'll, it'll help create more dialogue. So in a book called Exactly What to Say is where I learned that. And it's something that's just like weird. You don't know why that works, but I've tested it and it does. So after each of your tie downs, do a double. OK? Now, if you have four pillars, I would only do this two to three times. OK? Because if you keep asking, what are your thoughts on that?
